section.title: The Sermon on the Mount: The Narrow Way
section.sectionRationale: The Sermon closes by pressing for a decision. Jesus warns against hypocritical judging, invites bold prayer to a generous Father, sums up the law in the Golden Rule, and then sets two ways, two trees, two claims, and two houses side by side. The great teaching ends not with information but with a summons: hear these words and act on them, and build your life on the rock.
section.thesis: The kingdom life Jesus has described calls for a decisive response: to deal first with one's own sin, to ask a generous Father, to enter the narrow gate, to test teachers by their fruit, and to prove real discipleship not by saying Lord, Lord but by doing the Father's will, building one's whole life on Jesus' words.
section.frameNote: Eight beats: judging and the log in the eye; do not give what is holy to dogs; ask, seek, knock; the Golden Rule; the narrow and wide gates; false prophets known by fruit; those who say Lord, Lord; and the two builders, closing with the crowd's astonishment at Jesus' authority.

verses.text: “Do not {{krino|judge}}, or you will be judged.
For with the same judgment you pronounce, you will be judged, and with the measure you use, it will be measured to you.
Why do you look at the speck in your brother’s eye but fail to notice the beam in your own eye?
How can you say to your brother, ‘Let me take the speck out of your eye,’ while there is still a beam in your own eye?
You hypocrite! First take the beam out of your own eye, and then you will see clearly to remove the speck from your brother’s eye.
Do not give dogs what is holy; do not throw your pearls before swine. If you do, they may trample them under their feet, and then turn and tear you to pieces.

units.label: Ask, seek, knock
units.note: how much more will your Father give

verses.text: Ask, and it will be given to you; seek, and you will find; knock, and the door will be opened to you.
For everyone who asks receives; he who seeks finds; and to him who knocks, the door will be opened.
Which of you, if his son asks for bread, will give him a stone?
Or if he asks for a fish, will give him a snake?
So if you who are evil know how to give good gifts to your children, how much more will your Father in heaven give good things to those who ask Him!

units.label: The Golden Rule
units.note: the essence of the Law and the Prophets

verses.text: In everything, then, do to others as you would have them do to you. For this is the essence of the Law and the Prophets.
Enter through the {{stenos|narrow}} gate. For wide is the gate and broad is the way that leads to destruction, and many enter through it.
But small is the gate and narrow the way that leads to life, and only a few find it.
Beware of false prophets. They come to you in sheep’s clothing, but inwardly they are ravenous wolves.
By their {{karpos|fruit}} you will recognize them. Are grapes gathered from thornbushes, or figs from thistles?
Likewise, every good tree bears good fruit, but a bad tree bears bad fruit.
A good tree cannot bear bad fruit, and a bad tree cannot bear good fruit.
Every tree that does not bear good fruit is cut down and thrown into the fire.
So then, by their fruit you will recognize them.

units.label: Lord, Lord
units.note: not words but the Father's will

verses.text: Not everyone who says to Me, ‘Lord, Lord,’ will enter the kingdom of heaven, but only he who does the will of My Father in heaven.
Many will say to Me on that day, ‘Lord, Lord, did we not prophesy in Your name, and in Your name drive out demons and perform many miracles?’
Then I will tell them plainly, ‘I never knew you; depart from Me, you workers of lawlessness!’

units.label: The two builders
units.note: hear these words and act on them

verses.text: Therefore everyone who hears these words of Mine and acts on them is like a wise man who built his house on the rock.
The rain fell, the torrents raged, and the winds blew and beat against that house; yet it did not fall, because its foundation was on the rock.
But everyone who hears these words of Mine and does not act on them is like a foolish man who built his house on sand.
The rain fell, the torrents raged, and the winds blew and beat against that house, and it fell, and great was its collapse!”
When Jesus had finished saying these things, the crowds were astonished at His teaching,
because He taught as one who had {{exousia|authority}}, and not as their scribes.

krino.gloss: To judge, condemn, pass sentence. Jesus forbids the harsh, hypocritical judging that condemns others while ignoring one's own sin; verses 5 and 6 show He still calls for clear-eyed discernment once the log is removed.

stenos.gloss: Narrow, cramped, confined. The gate to life is narrow and found by few, while the broad road to destruction is crowded; the word presses the reader to a deliberate, costly choice rather than drifting with the many.

karpos.gloss: Fruit, produce, result. The test of a prophet, and of any life, is the fruit it bears. Words and appearances can deceive, but a person's actual character and works reveal the tree they truly are.

exousia.gloss: Authority, right, power. The crowds sense that Jesus teaches with an inherent authority the scribes lacked; He does not merely cite tradition but speaks as one whose own word is the foundation of life and the standard of judgment.

basic.context: Jesus brings the Sermon to a close by demanding a response. He warns against condemning others while ignoring our own faults, urges bold prayer to a generous Father, and gives the Golden Rule. Then He lays out stark choices: a narrow gate against a wide one, good trees against bad, true disciples who do God's will against those who only say Lord, Lord, and finally two builders whose houses stand or fall in the storm. The crowds are stunned at His authority.
basic.hermeneutics: Read do not judge in light of verses 5 and 6, which still call for discernment; Jesus forbids hypocritical condemnation, not all moral judgment. Read the closing images, gate, tree, claim, and house, as parallel calls to decision. The whole Sermon lands here: hearing Jesus' words is not enough; a wise life is built by acting on them.
basic.originalLanguages: Jesus forbids the harsh judging that condemns others while blind to our own sin. The gate to life is narrow, found by few, demanding a deliberate choice. A prophet, and any life, is known by its fruit, not its appearance. And the crowds marvel because Jesus teaches with an authority all His own, not the borrowed authority of the scribes.
basic.typology: The two ways echo the ancient choice set before Israel, life or death, blessing or curse. The tree and its fruit recall the prophets' tests of true and false teachers. And the storm that tests every house points to the final judgment, when what each life was built on will be revealed.
basic.christ: Jesus stands at the center as the one whose words are the rock, the standard by which every life stands or falls. He is the Judge who will say on the last day, I never knew you or depart from Me. He teaches with divine authority, and He is Himself the narrow gate and the way to life. To build on His words is to build on Him.
basic.god: The Father is the generous giver who delights to answer His children's prayers with good gifts. He is also the God whose will must be done, not merely professed, and whose judgment falls on every fruitless tree and sandy foundation. He is both lavishly kind and utterly holy.
basic.crossReferences: Deuteronomy 30 sets before Israel the two ways of life and death. Luke 6 gives a parallel form of much of this teaching. James 1 insists we be doers of the word, not hearers only. And Matthew 25 shows the final judgment where true and false are separated.
basic.oneStory: The greatest sermon ends with a fork in the road. Jesus has described a righteousness that reaches the heart and a life that trusts the Father, and now He asks: which way will you take, which foundation will you build on? The kingdom is entered not by admiring His teaching but by doing it, by building a whole life on the words of the King who speaks with the authority of God.

context.literary: This is the conclusion of the first discourse. It gathers a series of paired contrasts, two gates, two trees, two confessions, two builders, that force a decision, and it ends with the formula when Jesus had finished, which closes each of Matthew's five discourses. The crowd's astonishment at His authority frames the whole Sermon and prepares for the mighty deeds of chapters 8 and 9.
context.historical: The image of two ways was common in Jewish and early Christian teaching. Building on rock versus sand reflects the flash floods of the region, where a dry wadi could become a torrent. False prophets were a live danger, and the plea Lord, Lord with a list of ministry achievements reflects the reality that religious activity can mask an unchanged heart.
context.canonical: The two ways echo Deuteronomy 30:15-20 and Psalm 1, the way of life and the way of destruction. The fruit test and the warning against false prophets run through Scripture. The scene of judgment, Lord, Lord, I never knew you, anticipates Matthew 25 and the New Testament's insistence that genuine faith shows itself in obedience, James 2.

hermeneutics.authorIntent: To bring the Sermon to a decisive conclusion, calling hearers not merely to admire Jesus' words but to act on them, to enter the narrow way, to test teachers by their fruit, and to prove genuine discipleship by doing the Father's will and building life on the rock of Christ's words.
hermeneutics.descriptionVsPrescription: The closing section is strongly prescriptive, a series of commands and warnings. The parables of the trees and builders illustrate abiding truths about judgment and true discipleship. The promises about prayer are real assurances to God's children, framed by the Father's goodness, not blank checks detached from His will.

debates.question: Does do not judge forbid all moral evaluation?

views.label: It forbids hypocritical, condemning judgment
views.case: The very passage tells us to first remove our own log so we can see clearly to help a brother, and later to discern false prophets by their fruit, so Jesus targets censorious hypocrisy, not all discernment.

views.label: It warns that our standard rebounds on us
views.case: The stress falls on the measure you use being measured back; the command humbles us before God's judgment and checks a harsh, superior spirit, without abolishing wise moral judgment.

debates.bearing: Both agree Jesus condemns proud, hypocritical condemnation while still requiring humble discernment; we are to judge ourselves first and others gently and truthfully.

debates.question: How can those who prophesied and cast out demons hear I never knew you?

views.label: They had works without a true relationship
views.case: Impressive ministry and even apparent miracles are not proof of salvation; what saves is being known by Christ and doing the Father's will from a changed heart, which these never had.

views.label: Their lawlessness exposed a false faith
views.case: Jesus calls them workers of lawlessness; their ongoing disobedience showed that their confession Lord, Lord was empty, a bad tree whose fruit betrayed it.

debates.bearing: Both warn that religious activity can mask an unconverted heart; genuine faith is known by obedience and by being truly known by Christ, not by spectacular deeds.

study.originalLanguages: Jesus forbids the kind of krino that condemns a brother while ignoring the beam in one's own eye, yet the same passage calls for clear sight to help him and to spot ravenous wolves, so it is hypocritical judging He bans, not all discernment. The gate to life is stenos, narrow and cramped, found by few, a deliberate and costly choice against the crowded broad road. Prophets and lives alike are tested by their karpos, their fruit, since appearances deceive. And the crowds are astonished because Jesus teaches with exousia, an authority intrinsic to Himself, unlike the scribes who leaned on tradition.

typology.fulfillment: The choice of life or death set before Israel in Deuteronomy 30 and Psalm 1, now focused in the narrow gate that is Christ Himself.

typology.type: The tree known by its fruit
typology.fulfillment: The prophetic test of true and false teachers, fulfilled in the discernment Jesus' people exercise and in the final harvest of judgment.

typology.type: The house tested by the storm
typology.fulfillment: The day of judgment when every life's true foundation is revealed, standing only if built on the words of Christ.

study.christ: The end of the Sermon quietly makes an overwhelming claim about Jesus. He is the Judge who will pronounce the eternal verdict on that day, welcoming or banishing, and He is the one whose bare word, these words of Mine, is the rock on which a life either stands or collapses. No prophet ever spoke so; the scribes cited authorities, but Jesus is the authority. He is the narrow gate and the way to life, and to obey His teaching is to build on Him. The astonished crowds sense what the whole Gospel proclaims: this teacher speaks with the very authority of God.
study.god: The Father revealed here is boundlessly generous, delighting to give good gifts to children who ask, far more ready to bless than any earthly parent. Yet He is also holy and just: His will must be done, not merely professed; every fruitless tree is cut down; every house built on sand falls in the storm. The Sermon holds these together, a Father to be trusted and approached with bold, expectant prayer, and a God before whose judgment no empty confession or hypocritical religion will stand.

crossReferences.note: The two ways set before Israel, life and blessing or death and destruction, echoed in the narrow and wide gates.
The way of the righteous and the way of the wicked, and the tree that bears fruit, behind Jesus' imagery.
Be doers of the word and not hearers only, the very point of the two builders.
The final judgment in which the true and false are separated, foreshadowed by Lord, Lord, I never knew you.

study.oneStory: The Sermon ends where every hearer must: at a fork in the road. Jesus has unveiled the life of the kingdom, and now He presses for a verdict, narrow gate or wide, good tree or bad, true obedience or empty words, rock or sand. The kingdom is not entered by admiring His teaching but by acting on it, by building a whole life on the words of the King. And the astonished crowds glimpse the reason it matters so much: the one who speaks is no ordinary teacher but the Lord whose word is the foundation of the world and the standard of the last day.

quiz.q: What does Jesus mean by do not judge?

options.text: Do not condemn others hypocritically while ignoring your own sin; deal with your log first.
options.note: Verses 3-5 show He forbids hypocritical condemnation, then calls for clear-eyed help.

options.text: The standard you use on others will be applied to you.
options.note: True and part of His point in verse 2, but it understates the call to remove your own log and still discern rightly.

options.text: Never form any opinion about right and wrong in others.
options.note: The same passage calls for discernment about specks and false prophets, so this goes too far.

options.text: Moral standards do not matter to God at all.
options.note: The Sermon is full of moral standards; Jesus forbids hypocritical judging, not morality.

quiz.q: What does Jesus teach about asking God in prayer?

options.text: The Father delights to give good gifts to His children who ask, seek, and knock.
options.note: Verses 7-11 ground bold prayer in the Father's generous goodness.

options.text: God gives whatever we ask, exactly as we specify.
options.note: He gives good things to those who ask, but as a wise Father, not as a vending machine for every request.

options.text: We should ask rarely, so as not to trouble God.
options.note: Jesus urges persistent asking, seeking, and knocking, not restraint.

options.text: Prayer makes no difference to what God does.
options.note: The opposite; Jesus promises everyone who asks receives.

quiz.q: What is the point of the narrow and wide gates?

options.text: The way to life is narrow and found by few; entering it requires a deliberate choice.
options.note: Verses 13-14 contrast the crowded road to destruction with the narrow way to life.

options.text: Most people will naturally drift onto the road to life.
options.note: The opposite; many take the broad road, and only a few find the narrow way.

options.text: Both gates ultimately lead to the same destination.
options.note: Jesus says they lead to destruction and to life, two different destinies.

options.text: The gate to life is wide and easy to enter.
options.note: Jesus calls the gate to life narrow and small, not wide and easy.

quiz.q: Why will some who say Lord, Lord not enter the kingdom?

options.text: Because they did not do the Father's will; their words masked an unchanged heart.
options.note: Verses 21-23 show that confession and even miracles without obedience are empty.

options.text: Because they never performed any religious works at all.
options.note: In fact they claim prophecy, exorcism, and miracles; the lack was doing the Father's will, not activity.

options.text: Because they used the wrong words to address Jesus.
options.note: The issue is not the wording but the absence of true obedience and relationship.

options.text: Because entry to the kingdom is random and unknowable.
options.note: Jesus gives a clear criterion: doing the will of the Father, not random selection.

quiz.q: What is the difference between the two builders?

options.text: The wise one hears Jesus' words and acts on them; the foolish one hears but does not act.
options.note: Verses 24-27 turn on hearing plus doing versus hearing only.

options.text: The wise builder used better materials than the foolish one.
options.note: The difference is the foundation, obeying Jesus' words, not the building materials.

options.text: Only the foolish builder faced a storm.
options.note: Both houses faced the same storm; only the one on the rock stood.

options.text: The wise builder never heard Jesus' teaching at all.
options.note: Both heard; the wise one also acted on what he heard.

questions: Jesus tells us to remove the log from our own eye before the speck from a brother's. Where are you quick to criticize others while overlooking a larger fault in yourself?
The Father loves to give good gifts to those who ask. What have you stopped asking God for, and how might His fatherly generosity renew your prayers?
The way to life is narrow and found by few. In what area are you tempted to follow the crowd down the broad road rather than take the costly narrow way?
Jesus warns that saying Lord, Lord is not enough without doing the Father's will. Is there a place where your words about following Jesus outrun your actual obedience?
A wise life is built by hearing and doing Jesus' words. What is one teaching from this Sermon you have heard but not yet acted on, and what step will you take this week?

translationNotes.note: The Golden Rule, do to others as you would have them do to you, is called the essence, or sum, of the Law and the Prophets, echoing love your neighbor as yourself.
Workers of lawlessness renders a phrase for those who practice sin; despite their claims, their lives lacked the obedience that marks those Christ knows.
The source reads and it fell, followed by a dash and great was its collapse; the dash has been rendered as a comma for a clean, dash-free reading.

meta.notes: BSB text fetched this session from bible.helloao.org for Matthew 7:1-29. Verse text assembled by concatenating segment values in order; line-break markers were skipped. The em dash in verse 27 (and it fell, and great was its collapse) was converted to a comma. No em or en dashes appear. Sermon left as none; no expositor sermon URL verified this session.
